name: ip-directed-search
description: Systematic IP licensing target discovery and sequencing. Scans every sector for companies deploying qualifying AI systems (Family A actuation governance, Family B learning governance), scores each target on deal size, regulatory urgency, accessibility, and response probability, then builds the master 90-day outreach sequence — who gets contacted in what order, through what channel, on what date. Feed it a sector and patent context — get back a ranked attack list and a day-by-day campaign calendar ready to execute.

config.instruction: Scan the target sector exhaustively for companies deploying AI systems that qualify under Family A (runtime actuation governance) or Family B (runtime learning governance). SIGNAL TYPE 1 — Job postings: search for ML reliability engineer, AI governance lead, model risk manager, responsible AI, MLOps, autonomy safety engineer. A company hiring for these roles has the system. SIGNAL TYPE 2 — Press releases: find companies using language like 'continuously learning', 'adaptive underwriting', 'dynamic pricing engine', 'real-time risk assessment', 'model updates automatically'. SIGNAL TYPE 3 — Regulatory filings: SEC 10-K filings mentioning adaptive AI (public companies), NAIC filings for insurance, FDA 510(k) clearances mentioning adaptive algorithms, California DMV AV testing reports. SIGNAL TYPE 4 — Conference appearances: find company employees speaking on adaptive AI, model governance, AI reliability, regulatory compliance for AI at sector-relevant conferences. These are the champions. SIGNAL TYPE 5 — Patent filings: companies filing adjacent patents on model monitoring, governance, constraint — they know the space and are implementing something. SIGNAL TYPE 6 — Engineering blogs and technical publications: companies publishing on model drift, data drift, rollback, governance are actively thinking about the problem. For each company found: document the AI system description, qualifying signal, signal sources, Family A exposure, Family B exposure, and confidence score (HIGH=confirmed deployment, MEDIUM=strong signals, LOW=inferred). Also compile sector intelligence: regulatory clock, enforcement actions, upcoming deadlines, public statements about AI governance gaps.

Take raw_targets and qualify every company against the specific patent claims. CLAIM MAPPING: For each company, map their known AI system to specific Family A and B claim triggers. Family B triggers: system adapts parameters on live data (Claim 1), monitors behavioral drift (Claims 4-6), has trust/confidence score that degrades (Claims 7-9), can roll back to prior state (Claims 17-18), maintains audit log (Claims 20-23). Family A triggers: actuates physical or decisioning outputs based on AI, has safety envelope, has non-bypassable constraint layer. DEAL SIZE: Estimate revenue base attributable to the AI system. Apply royalty rate 0.08-0.15% for annual royalty. Add upfront estimate based on company size. Calculate 5-year LTV. URGENCY SCORE (1-10): Regulatory deadline proximity (30%), regulator has issued specific guidance (20%), peer already licensed (20%), recent enforcement action (15%), company acknowledged gap publicly (15%). ACCESSIBILITY SCORE (1-10): Warm path exists (35%), decision-maker identified (20%), decision-maker publicly active (15%), company size mid-market (15%), prior licensing history (15%). RESPONSE SCORE (1-10): Regulatory pressure acute (25%), technical team engaged in governance topics (20%), GC or CRO spoken publicly on AI risk (20%), company raising capital (15%), M&A activity (10%), clean slate (10%). OVERALL SCORE: deal_size_normalized×0.30 + urgency×0.25 + accessibility×0.25 + response×0.20. A-List = score ≥ 7.0 AND deal size ≥ $1M LTV. B-List = score 4.0-6.9. Disqualify: no qualifying signal, pre-revenue, active IP litigation defense, explicit open-source-only AI stance. For all A-List targets: cross-reference licensor network against target investors, board, advisors, alumni for warm paths.

Take scored_targets (qualified_targets, a_list, b_list) and build the master 90-day outreach sequence. SEQUENCING RULES: (1) Highest urgency_score goes first regardless of other factors. (2) Sector stagger — minimum 30-day gap between outreach starts for direct competitors in the same sector. Never let two sector competitors know you're talking to both simultaneously. (3) Warm paths within a sector jump to top of sector queue — activate warm intro 7 days before first cold touch. (4) Never run more than 3 active first-touch sequences simultaneously (Days 1-22 per target). After Day 22 a target moves to monitoring and a new one activates. (5) Align outreach timing to trigger events — touch 3-4 should land 7-14 days before a major regulatory deadline or conference where the target is present. CALENDAR: Build week-by-week view showing active sequences, warming targets, queued targets, and response status. TRIGGER EVENTS: Map every regulatory deadline, earnings call, funding round, leadership change, product launch, and industry conference to the targets they affect with optimal outreach timing. DASHBOARD: Build the one-page 90-day campaign view with ranked A-list, sector stagger map, trigger event calendar, and pipeline status. NOTE: The output of this step feeds directly into ip-licensing-outreach — for each A-List target in sequence order, the full outreach chain runs with that target's company, contact, and deal angle.

Build the master execution checklist for the directed search campaign using all upstream results. SECTION 1 — INTELLIGENCE VALIDATION: For each A-List target, confirm qualifying signal is current (job posting still live, press release still accessible, regulatory filing confirmed). Flag any targets where signals are >6 months old as 'stale — re-verify before outreach.' SECTION 2 — WARM PATH ACTIVATION: For each STRONG or MEDIUM warm path identified, list: connector name/role, activation message, send date (7 days before target's outreach start), expected response window. Action: send activation messages on the dates specified. SECTION 3 — PRE-OUTREACH PREP PER TARGET: For each A-List target in sequence order — confirm GC or CRO name and current role (LinkedIn check day before outreach), verify email format via Hunter.io, confirm no prior contact in target_registry, confirm deal brief is ready for this field of use. SECTION 4 — OUTREACH TRIGGER: For each target, the outreach trigger date from campaign_sequence. On that date, run ip-licensing-outreach chain with that target's context. SECTION 5 — SECTOR STAGGER ENFORCEMENT: List each sector with its stagger map. Flag any violation — two companies in the same sector within 30 days — and recommend resolution. SECTION 6 — TRIGGER EVENT ALERTS: Calendar of regulatory deadlines, conferences, and company events with the target they affect and the action required (accelerate outreach, prepare conference approach, monitor earnings call). SECTION 7 — PIPELINE REVIEW CADENCE: Weekly 15-minute review — what's active, what responded, what needs follow-up, what's entering the sequence. Monthly review — A-List vs B-List performance, which signal types predicted response best, adjust scoring weights. Output as a structured checklist with action, target, date, channel, and success criteria for every item.
